Got My First Job! No VCS, No Testing, No Docs, No Code Reviews, Code Directly Written in Prod | I don't really know why I'm posting this or what I'm expecting but I recently got my first dev job with a very small team, so small that each individual basically has their own project with no overlap on anyone else's work, so there isn't really any collaboration. 

There's no Version Control, No dev, testing or staging servers. Everything is written in prod (even database stuff), and immediately just copied over to another directory where all the live code is run. No development lifecycle or CI/CD to speak of and barely any documentation. 

This is my first job so I feel insanely discouraged. I was so hyped but I don't really even know what to feel now. I was actually excited about feeling overwhelmed with all this new stuff I'd have to learn but right now it basically just feels like I'm getting paid to work on personal projects since there aren't any code reviews or really any mentoring (I'm also using the newest tech of anyone in the team so I'm the only one that knows it).

I've been thinking that since there isn't really any overlap between anyone's projects, I should take the initiative to create a git repository and document everything as best I can I doubt I'll be able to implement any kind of CI/CD though. 

I would leave but I think I'm going to have to stick this out until after I pay off my loans and covid ends, given the job market.

To top it off the people I work with are seriously some of the coolest people I've ever met. 

Kudos to anyone that read this monologue of me venting lol

Normal to feel like a 'breakup' after leaving a company? | Left my first software job a few months back. They taught me everything I knew and I spent a lot of time with my team who I grew to like very much (and I don't think I realized how close we were). We did great work together but I ended up leaving for a somewhat higher-paying job at a company I now know to be far more boring, pretentious, and lonely in terms of team interaction.

So basically the transition has had some 'breakup' qualities - I think about my old company almost every day, sometimes dream about it, sometimes lose sleep wishing I hadn't jumped ship on them. I've hung out with them a few times since and I generally badly want to tell them that I want to come back lol. 

I feel like it might be a 'grass is greener on the other side' mentality as there were some negative things about working there, but I'm unsure if I miss them too much, or more than normal?

How do I handle my “boss” (at a position I don’t even get paid for) being rude to me on the second day? | I started a volunteer position today which has the ability to turn into a job. My “boss” asked me make a poster and I did. Now keep in mind my graduate degree gave me extensive graphics training. And I’ve done this job paid multiple times before and always was told I was a fantastic employee. She gave me very few details about this poster. Then she gets snippy about how the poster isn’t right and how she wants a discussion about my experience level. Now IF I was being paid I wouldn’t care so much BUT for this to be unpaid and for her to be flat out rude when she really didn’t give me enough details makes me worry. I mean how can you expect someone to stick around when you act like a jerk? And also at her level of leadership bad communication also worries me. It’s definitely something that makes me lose respect.
Edit: I lost my old job due to COVID and decided to volunteer while I’m looking for something new.
Update: Thank you everyone who provided help. I quit this morning via email effective immediately. I really appreciate everyone’s help.

My next phone interview will be this coming Thursday and I'm wondering how to talk about my relocation. Should I lie and say I'm in the area and that I have a planned vacation and can't work right away, should I just avoid the topic altogether, or should I be honest and risk being ghosted again??

Underqualified for most dev jobs - Update my skills or grind leetcode? | Hi everyone, 

I have a bachelor's degree in CS and 2 years of experience in an amazing mid-size product company making Windows desktop apps. The tech stack is quite outdated compared to the current market standards (C#, UWP, XAML). We don't follow industry standard practices such writing unit tests or Agile. 

While searching for jobs, most of them seem to be asking for experience in front-end frameworks Angular/React along with knowledge in skills like unit testing, ASP, Spring, Hibernate etc even for junior positions. I just have a basic knowledge of HTML, CSS and vanilla JS and Java but am quite proficient with just C# which doesn't seem to be enough. I have not been getting too many callbacks but I did get a couple of interviews even though I was very very underqualified for their position. In my last 2 interviews when I mentioned  I have never written a unit test their reaction was basically "Oh? Thats ummm interesting to hear...". And most other positions don't even seem to even give me a coding test or a phone screen.

Now, should I focus on learning these skills that are hot in the market, or should I focus on grinding leetcode and my data structures? Please help.

Word of encouragement | I recently got an offer from a Big N company, and I am sitting here a bit dumbfounded. It is honestly something I never dreamed of achieving. 

There were many many interviews that I have experienced so far, and a very small handful has been successful, while most of them were flops. I dont think I necessarily did better in the ones that I got an offer for, but nevertheless they wanted me. The only thing that I really can confidently say is that I tried my best at every interview. I felt slightly nervous during each one, let out a huge sigh of relief after every single call, and continued to practice leetcode as much as I can. This time, things just aligned well, even though I just did what I usually did.

For those who are frustrated with trying to catch elusive success, dont worry. If you continue to do your best with integrity and hardwork, it will pay off. Your time will come, and I hope that you will find joy and satisfaction in knowing that what you are doing everyday is helping you grow whether you get an offer or not.

tldr: 
the hard work that you are doing now is of value, and someday the world will take notice.

EDIT:
Getting a Big N company was a personal dream and goal of mine, but it does not have to be for you :) Getting a Big N company or NOT getting one should never be a representation of your worth.

“Gifted burnout slacker” gets a job offer after 11 months of grinding and applying | I graduated university by leeching off my friends in coding projects and just memorized enough to get my diploma; I had ZERO coding experience/aptitude.

My programming journey started by doing LeetCode questions and I just felt incredibly stupid. I eventually was able to understand the logic behind 1 question but I didnt feel like I really progressed. 

Then I went to Udemy to do some online courses aaaaaaand yup. Tutorial Honeymoon phase. I felt like I was progressing and doing actual programming. Once I wanted to try to do what I’ve learned on my own, I realized, I had no idea how to start.

Once I decided to make my own projects, (and I emphasize that this is the crucial turning point to start the upswing) things started to click and connect. 

I started by literally spending a whole day just memorizing how to create a project:
npx create-react-app [project name], cd [project name], npm start. Then slowly but surely, I Googled and YouTubed what I wanted to do. It was a simple React Counting app, then I wanted to learn Hooks, then I wanted to learn CSS... it just kept snowballing. Before I knew it, I had my own portfolio website, a couple of projects I’ve completed on my own and I felt ready to apply. 

Applying is a numbers game. I applied to 10 jobs a day for 3 months straight. I’ve gotten maybe 15 calls in those 3 months. Of the 15 calls, 6 of them gave me a chance with a technical test, and of those 6 chances, I’ve failed 5 of them. The one I didnt fail? I made it to the final round of interviewing only to lose because someone else just had more experience than me. 

I was tired and drained but not too long after that final round silver medal, I got an email offer.

I’ve never been so happy to get an offer in my life. All the struggle was worth it. I would also like to say that I identify as a “gifted burnout” and I did therapy to help me overcome my “child prodigy” ego or whatever you wanna call it. 

If you feel like you’re past your prime or you cant learn to program, learn from a gifted-burnout-slacker like me. You CAN. Believe in yourself. Keep going. Its a marathon, and I’ll see you on the other side of the finish line.

Prevent Burnout - its common! | Hey guys, one of the most common posts I see at least once a day is about burn-out, wanting to quit, not liking their job, etc. - even myself I have posted about being burnt out and being overworked.

It's sad that it's so prevalent. As programmers, we are the REAL workers actually ENGINEERING and INVENTING our companies' products and services - and the non-technicals think it's all easy-peasy work and that we're all just 'wizards' that type stuff.

It's straight up not. It's mentally stressful - thinking of an entire application's logical structure, organization, team members, refactoring, deadlines, more refactoring, features, git, deployment, design, testing, merge conflicts, frameworks, bugs, etc, it goes on. - and can be physically demanding - no sleep, no eating, lots of caffeine, hygiene problems, eye problems, back/neck problems etc - all in the name of 'getting work done'.

For those that have been burnt out, how did you get through it? How do you prevent it? How can I see before entering a job if burnout is real in that company?

I want to see less burn out posts on here and more happy stuff. lets get this merged.

​

EDIT: 

Thanks for commenting for those that did - I see a common theme among the comments:

\- Truly Dedicate Hours to Work and hours to free time - like no touching computer/coding at all in your free time.

\- Dont take on too many tasks - humans can work on one thing at a time. Keep at least a basic to-do list and if a new task is going to you - say no that you are too busy; no time. It's ok

\- Know your work environment - are your teammates feeling burnt out too? or your future/possible team mates? Talk with them to make sure before their work gets dumped on you!

Accepted an offer for a new job, after months of searching! Stay positive! | After nearly 10 months of being unemployed, I finally got a new job. It has been a rollercoaster ride with the COVID pandemic. I lost my first full-time job after graduating, which was working at the front desk of a FinTech company.

Since July, I had been collecting unemployment and applying to jobs on a weekly basis and trying to find any source of work that will pay me more than unemployment does now. I finally found a job working for an after school program at a school, something I had done previously for 4 years. It's only part-time at 30 hours a week, with no benefits, but it pays more than unemployment does, so it is a start!

For me, I am just taking a deep breath and not worrying about anyone else, or whether they kept their job, or how much someone else gets paid, and whether it's more than me or not. With the COVID-19 pandemic, many people are going to have to take a step back and take on roles that aren't in their field or that they are overqualified for.

Definitely nervous about starting a new job though. I haven't had very much social interaction(other than recruiters/interviews), or really any other social contact outside my inner circle. Trying to stay positive through all of it though! I got through a lot of pain and suffering during this pandemic and you will too!

​

EDIT: I graduated with a degree in Marketing. I've applied just about everywhere, and I showcased my portfolio, with no answers from any recruiters. But a lot of my previous experience before graduation had been in education, so here I am "working backward".

I finally landed a job. I told myself I wasn't gonna do this kind of a post, but here goes: | TL;WR After an 18 month search I finally landed a job. As cliché as it sounds, don't give up!

As recent as yesterday I was still getting annoyed whenever someone posted to this sub stating they landed a job. I saw it as bragging, and maybe that's what I'm doing here but not trying to. Rather I'll tell you my job hunting story to try to instill some hope and validate your feelings. 

Everyone frustrated with the seamingly endless job search experiences some level of anxiety and depression. It starts as simply fear of the unknown and decreased sense of self worth. Lack of employment quickly plays a heavy toll on your mental well-being. Your mind starts to become poisoned with negativity and dread about the future. It's really easy to slip into a skewed perspective and see everything as hopeless. Maybe not at first, but dozens or even hundreds of applications into the process and no amount of confidence can stand that much rejection.  

The human brain is resilient and adaptive, but with diminishing returns and as time goes on wants turn into needs and eventually, desperation. This job market is brutal and nothing is fair or equitable. I feel my mind has become numb to emotion through this process to cope with rejection and feeling powerless. I am grateful I finally landed a job but it shouldn't have been this hard. 

I applied to over 400 places, 78 interviews, three rescinded offers, but finally signed an offer letter yesterday. I know nothing is completely secure (I could still get laid off or lose my job) but I feel hope again for the first time in a long time. 

All that to say, your feelings are normal and valid, but don't give up. Persistence is key and resilience is how you will land the job. Don't let the rejection own you.

I bombed out | I made it to the 3rd round, then bombed out.

My mistake -- I didn't study everything I put on my resume.

The new trend these days is to be a leetcode master. But not every company put so much stock in leetcode, because really leetcode only makes sense for FAANG. But many companies love leetcode anyway.

So there are now two styles of interviews to prepare for -- leetcode, and traditional. I respect the traditional approach, and I think leetcode is bullshit (you can disagree, some people believe in leetcode and that's fine), but now we have two paths to prepare for.

I've been practicing leetcode everyday that I didn't wok on the traditional stuff, which is to know what you put on your resume very well.

I have things on there from years ago. It's not uncommon to work on one thing because your boss told you to, so you did your research and knocked it out. Then you never touch it again. Someone asks you about it 5 years later... um... yeah I did that, but I don't remember what I did.

They were nice, they keep pulling back the question to more and more generic because I don't remember. But that's just the interviewers being nice, being nice != job.

So moral of the story, sure, grind that leetcode like your life depends on it. But before the interview, make sure you go back and study everything you put on your resume.

Oh well. It is what it is. I haven't interviewed for a long time. First one usually turns out pretty bad. I remember awhile back before leetcode was a thing, I was interviewing quite a bit when I was looking. Answers were just flying out of my mouth because interviewers tend to ask the same questions. Like everything else in life, the more you do it, the better you get at it. Maybe I should start doing those mock interviews.

Best of luck to you all. To me too, although I'd put it towards winning the lotto. Yes, I know the odd. It's what happens when desperation > math knowledge.
